detailed_units.title: Maupay nga Aga!
detailed_units.can_do: I can greet people, introduce myself simply, and say thanks.

new_grammar.explain_en: The linker 'nga' connects an adjective to a noun or certain adverbs to verbs. It's often used in greetings like 'good morning'.
The marker 'an' functions like 'the' or 'a/an' for common nouns. It often introduces the topic of a sentence.

questions.q: If someone says 'Kamusta ka?', how might you respond?

detailed_units.title: Hain an Balay?
detailed_units.can_do: I can ask where things or people are, state 'here' or 'there', and identify family members.

new_grammar.explain_en: The marker 'hi' is used before personal names (or titles used as names) when they are the topic or subject of the sentence.
The marker 'ha' indicates location or direction, similar to 'at', 'in', or 'to'.

examples.en: He/she is here at the house.

new_vocab.note: A specific location in Daram, Samar.

sentences.en: He is here at the house.
He/she is over there in Balite.

questions.q: Who is 'hiya' in 'Didto hiya ha Balite'?

detailed_units.title: Gusto Ko Tubig
detailed_units.can_do: I can express what I want, and ask for food or drink politely.

new_grammar.explain_en: To say 'I want' or 'I like', you use 'gusto' followed by the enclitic pronoun '-ko' (for 'I'). For 'you want', use '-mo'.
Waray verbs can often be used directly. For simple commands or statements of intention, the root verb is common.

detailed_units.title: Akon nga Balay
detailed_units.can_do: I can talk about 'my', 'your', and 'his/her' possessions.

new_grammar.explain_en: These pronouns come before the noun they modify, indicating possession. They act like adjectives: 'my house'.
These short pronouns attach to the end of a word, often a noun for possession ('house my') or a verb/adjective to indicate the actor/agent ('want I').

sentences.war: Gusto ni Nanay an iya tubig.

detailed_units.title: Pira Ini?
detailed_units.can_do: I can count small numbers, ask 'how many' or 'how much', and buy simple items.

new_grammar.explain_en: 'Pira' is used to ask 'how many' or 'how much' when referring to quantity.

examples.en: How many children do you have?

new_grammar.explain_en: 'Tagpira' specifically asks for the price per item or unit.

examples.en: How much is the rice (per serving)?

new_grammar.explain_en: The prefix 'ma-' can indicate a future action or intention. 'Mapalit' means 'will buy' or 'is going to buy'.

new_vocab.note: Root form, often used as imperative.

questions.q: How many waters will the person buy?
What is the price for one water?

detailed_units.title: Yana nga Adlaw
detailed_units.can_do: I can talk about 'now', 'today', 'yesterday', and 'tomorrow', and ask 'when?'.

new_grammar.explain_en: The marker 'ha' can also indicate 'at' or 'on' for specific times or general periods.
The phrase 'yana nga' is used to specify 'this' or 'today' with time nouns.

questions.q: When will the speaker buy water?

detailed_units.title: Kita Ngan Kamo
detailed_units.can_do: I can refer to groups of people using 'we' (inclusive/exclusive), 'you all', and 'they'.

new_grammar.explain_en: 'Kita' (independent), 'aton' (pre-posed), and '-ta' (enclitic) mean 'we' *including* the listener. 'Kami' (independent), 'amon' (pre-posed), and '-mi' (enclitic) mean 'we' *excluding* the listener.

new_vocab.gloss: we (exclusive, I and others, not you)

new_vocab.note: Enclitic possessive/agent pronoun. 'niyo' is common after nouns.
Enclitic possessive/agent pronoun. 'nira' is common after nouns.

sentences.en: We (not you) will buy cooked rice tomorrow.
Let's eat this noon (you and I).

sentences.war: Didto an ira balay ha Balite.
sentences.en: Their house is over there in Balite.

questions.q: Who will buy cooked rice tomorrow?
